---
title: "Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip"
subtitle: "What to bring from the US for your electronics"
author: Dan Green
date: 10/11/25
format:
  html:
    theme: lux
    toc: true
---

## Quick Summary

**Bottom line:** You'll need adapters for Korea but not Japan. Most modern electronics will work without voltage converters in both countries.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Voltage & Frequency

| Country           | Voltage | Frequency  | US Compatibility       |
|-------------------|---------|------------|------------------------|
| **United States** | 120V    | 60 Hz      | \-                     |
| **Japan**         | 100V    | 50/60 Hz\* | Generally compatible   |
| **South Korea**   | 220V    | 60 Hz      | Need adapter/converter |

\*Eastern Japan (Tokyo) uses 50 Hz; Western Japan (Osaka, Kyoto) uses 60 Hz

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Plug Types

### Japan

-   **Type A & B** (same as US)
-   Your US plugs will fit directly into Japanese outlets
-   No adapter needed

### South Korea

-   **Type C & F** (European-style round pins)
-   Your US plugs will NOT fit
-   Adapter required

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## What You Need to Bring

### Essential: Universal Travel Adapter

Bring at least **one universal travel adapter** that includes: - Type C/F plugs for Korea - Multiple USB ports (very helpful)

**Recommended brands:** - EPICKA Universal Travel Adapter - TESSAN Universal Travel Adapter - Anker USB Wall Charger with international adapters

### Optional: Power Strip

Consider bringing a **US power strip** (3-4 outlets): - Plug the strip into your adapter in Korea - Allows you to charge multiple US devices from one adapter - Works with your existing US charging cables - Only need ONE adapter this way

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Your Devices: What Works Where

### ✅ No Converter Needed (Just Adapter for Korea)

Most modern electronics have dual-voltage power supplies (100-240V):

-   **Laptops** - Check the power brick; it should say "100-240V"
-   **Phones** - All modern smartphones are dual voltage
-   **Tablets** (iPad, etc.) - Dual voltage
-   **Camera battery chargers** - Usually dual voltage
-   **Electric toothbrush chargers** - Check label
-   **Power banks** - No issue (they charge via USB)

**How to check:** Look for "INPUT: 100-240V" on the device or power adapter

### ⚠️ May Need Converter

High-wattage heat-producing devices (rarely dual voltage):

-   Hair dryers
-   Curling irons
-   Straighteners

**Recommendation:** Don't bring these. Buy cheap ones in Korea/Japan if needed, or use hotel-provided hair dryers.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Practical Tips

### In Japan

1.  Your plugs fit directly - no adapter needed
2.  Some older Japanese outlets only accept 2-prong (Type A), not 3-prong (Type B)
3.  Voltage is slightly lower (100V vs US 120V) but this is fine for modern electronics

### In Korea

1.  Bring 1-2 adapters, or 1 adapter + US power strip
2.  Hotels often provide adapters at the front desk (ask!)
3.  Convenience stores (GS25, CU, 7-Eleven) sell adapters if needed

### General

-   Download offline maps before you go
-   Consider bringing a portable charger (10,000+ mAh)
-   Many cafes and convenience stores have charging spots
-   Trains and buses often have USB charging ports

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Shopping List

### Must Have

-   [ ] 1-2 universal travel adapters with Type C/F

### Nice to Have

-   [ ] US power strip (3-4 outlets)
-   [ ] Portable battery bank (10,000-20,000 mAh)
-   [ ] Extra USB charging cables
-   [ ] USB wall adapter with multiple ports

### Don't Bother

-   [ ] ~~Voltage converter~~ (unless you have specific 120V-only devices)
-   [ ] ~~Hair dryer/straightener~~ (use hotel's or buy there)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Questions to Ask Yourself

Before packing, check your devices:

1.  Does my laptop charger say "100-240V"? (It almost certainly does)
2.  How many devices will I need to charge simultaneously?
3.  Will I need to work/charge in cafes or only in hotels?
4.  Is my portable charger fully charged?

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Resources

-   [Japan voltage info](https://www.japan-guide.com)
-   [Korea travel tips](https://english.visitkorea.or.kr)

**Have a great trip!**
